---
title: "Population Regulation"
subtitle: "Lecture 4 --- Density dependence and its consequences"
institute: "Odum School of Ecology"
event: "ECOL 3500 --- Ecology"
location: "Room 200, Biological Sciences"
date: today
format:
  drake-teach-coral-revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    transition: none
    incremental: false
---

## Where we left off

Last time we introduced exponential growth:

$$N_{t+1} = \lambda N_t$$

This model predicts unbounded growth. Today we ask:
**what stops populations from growing forever?**

## Density dependence

The per-capita growth rate declines as population size increases.

- **Food limitation** --- fewer resources per individual
- **Space** --- territorial animals exclude newcomers
- **Disease** --- transmission increases with density
- **Predation** --- predators aggregate where prey are dense

### All of these create negative feedback on growth

## The logistic model

Adding a carrying capacity $K$ to the exponential model:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

- When $N \ll K$: growth is approximately exponential
- When $N = K$: growth stops
- When $N > K$: population declines

## Growth rate vs. density {.figure}

![](figures/fig-invasion-curves-1.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Per-capita growth rate as a function of population density in two Dreissenid mussel species.</p>

# Equilibria {.transition}

What happens in the long run?

## Finding equilibria

Set $dN/dt = 0$ and solve:

$$rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) = 0$$

Two solutions:

1. $N^* = 0$ (extinction)
2. $N^* = K$ (carrying capacity)

### $K$ is a stable equilibrium; $0$ is unstable

## Stability analysis

```r
library(deSolve)

logistic <- function(t, y, parms) {
  with(as.list(c(y, parms)), {
    dN <- r * N * (1 - N / K)
    list(c(dN = dN))
  })
}

parms <- c(r = 0.5, K = 500)
times <- seq(0, 40, by = 0.1)

# Start from different initial conditions
N0_values <- c(10, 50, 200, 800)
```

## Logistic dynamics {.figure-slide}

![](figures/sir-epidemic.png){fig-align="center"}

<p class="caption">Solutions of the logistic equation for four initial population sizes. All converge to $K = 500$.</p>

## Key parameters

| Parameter | Symbol  | Meaning                              |
|-----------|---------|--------------------------------------|
| Growth rate | $r$   | Maximum per-capita rate of increase  |
| Carrying capacity | $K$ | Equilibrium population size      |
| Doubling time | $\ln 2 / r$ | Time to double when $N \ll K$ |

## A quote to remember

> The struggle for existence inevitably follows from the high
> rate at which all organic beings tend to increase.
>
> --- Charles Darwin, *On the Origin of Species*

## Field study {background-image="figures/photo.png" background-size="cover"}

::: {.frosted}
Long-term population monitoring at the Coweeta Hydrologic
Laboratory provides data on density-dependent dynamics
in forest communities.
:::

## {.full-image background-image="figures/photo.png" background-size="cover" background-position="center"}

::: {.full-image-label}
Coweeta LTER
:::

## Teaching team {.collaborators}

::: {.people}

::: {.person}
![](figures/person1.png)

Sarah Chen (TA)
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person2.png)

Marcus Williams (TA)
:::

::: {.person}
![](figures/person3.png)

Priya Patel (ULA)
:::

:::

## Logistics {.watermark background-image="figures/watermark.png" background-size="contain" background-position="center" background-opacity="0.08"}

- Problem Set 3 is due Friday at 5 pm on eLC
- Lab this week: fitting the logistic model to data in R
- Office hours: Thursday 2--3 pm, Ecology 411

# Next time {.divider}

*Competition and the Lotka--Volterra model*
